title: Why we still mend things
summary: A short editorial that defends repair cafés — not for the usual environmental reasons, but for the kind of attention that mending teaches.
body:
There is a small movement in some European cities to mend things rather than replace them. The movement has different names — repair cafés in the Netherlands, fixing workshops in Germany, mending circles in Britain. The idea is unremarkable on its face. People meet on Saturday afternoons in church halls or community centres. Someone brings a broken kettle. Someone else brings a torn coat. A pair of volunteers, who used to work as engineers or tailors, tries to fix the thing, often with the owner watching.

A defender of mending will list the obvious benefits. Less waste in landfill. Less money spent. Less material pulled out of the earth for new products. All of these are real, and all of them have been made many times in the press. They are usually enough to convince an editor to commission an article about repair cafés. They are usually not enough to make a young person carry a kettle to a church hall instead of ordering a new one online.

So let me offer a less obvious defence. Mending is not only about objects. It is also about a habit of attention that the wider culture has begun to lose. When you mend something, you must first understand how it was put together. You must look inside. You must accept that the broken part is small and the working part is large. You must respect, briefly, the person who designed it. None of this happens when you replace a thing.

This habit of attention spreads. People who mend their kettles often start mending other things. They start mending their relationships. They start mending old photographs. They start mending sentences in their own writing. The mending café is a school whose first lesson is the kettle and whose later lessons are quieter.

There are limits, of course. Some things should not be mended. Old electrical wiring is dangerous. A car's brakes are not for amateurs. A medical device must be left to professionals. The argument for mending is not an argument for amateur expertise in every field. It is an argument for the small daily judgment: this object is mendable; let me try.

The objection is sometimes raised that mending wastes time. A kettle costs forty euros. An hour at a repair café costs you that hour. If your hour is worth more than forty euros to you, replacing is rational. But this objection assumes that the hour spent mending is empty time. It is not. It is time spent learning, time spent in company, and time spent — quietly, and without anyone announcing it — refusing to live in a world where everything is disposable.

The kettle, in the end, is almost beside the point.

annotations.meaningVi: chuyên môn không chuyên nghiệp / kiến thức nghiệp dư
dùng một lần / dễ vứt bỏ
không phải vấn đề chính / lạc đề

grammarNotes.title: Modal 'must' for the necessary conditions of an argument
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn nghị luận B1, 'must + động từ nguyên thể' thường dùng để liệt kê những điều bắt buộc của một quá trình hoặc lập luận — không phải mệnh lệnh cho người đọc, mà là mô tả 'điều phải xảy ra'. Bài có chuỗi: 'you must first understand how it was put together. You must look inside. You must accept that the broken part is small… You must respect, briefly, the person who designed it.' Mỗi 'you must' là một bước bắt buộc của hành động sửa chữa. So sánh 'should' (nên — gợi ý nhẹ) yếu hơn 'must' (phải — điều kiện thiết yếu).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In B1 argument, 'must + base verb' often lists necessary conditions of a process — it is not a command to the reader but a description of what has to happen. The passage stacks them: 'you must first understand… You must look inside. You must accept… You must respect…' Each 'you must' is a required step. Compare 'should' (a softer suggestion) — 'must' marks a true requirement.

translationVi:
Ở một vài thành phố châu Âu có một phong trào nhỏ: sửa đồ thay vì thay đồ. Phong trào có nhiều tên — 'quán cà phê sửa chữa' ở Hà Lan, 'xưởng vá víu' ở Đức, 'vòng tròn vá đồ' ở Anh. Ý tưởng tự nó không có gì lạ. Người ta gặp nhau chiều thứ Bảy trong sảnh nhà thờ hoặc trung tâm cộng đồng. Có người mang một cái ấm hỏng. Người khác mang một cái áo rách. Một cặp tình nguyện viên — từng là kỹ sư hoặc thợ may — thử sửa, thường có chủ đồ đứng xem.

Một người ủng hộ việc sửa chữa sẽ liệt kê những lợi ích quen thuộc. Giảm rác chôn lấp. Bớt tiền chi. Bớt nguyên liệu phải khai thác cho sản phẩm mới. Tất cả đều đúng, và tất cả đều đã được lặp lại nhiều lần trên báo chí. Chúng đủ để thuyết phục một biên tập viên đặt viết một bài về 'repair café'. Chúng thường không đủ để khiến một người trẻ bê cái ấm đến sảnh nhà thờ thay vì đặt mua online một cái mới.

Vậy hãy để tôi đưa ra một lập luận ít hiển nhiên hơn. Việc sửa chữa không chỉ nói về đồ vật. Nó còn nói về một kiểu chú ý mà văn hoá rộng hơn đã bắt đầu đánh mất. Khi bạn sửa một thứ, bạn phải hiểu trước xem nó đã được ghép vào với nhau ra sao. Bạn phải nhìn vào bên trong. Bạn phải chấp nhận rằng phần hỏng nhỏ, phần đang hoạt động lớn. Bạn phải tôn trọng, trong một khoảnh khắc, người đã thiết kế ra nó. Không điều nào trong số này diễn ra khi bạn thay mới.

Thói quen chú ý này lan rộng. Người sửa được cái ấm thường bắt đầu sửa thứ khác. Họ bắt đầu sửa các mối quan hệ. Họ bắt đầu phục chế ảnh cũ. Họ bắt đầu sửa câu trong bài viết của chính mình. 'Repair café' là một ngôi trường mà bài đầu tiên là cái ấm và những bài về sau thì yên ắng hơn.

Dĩ nhiên có giới hạn. Một số thứ không nên tự sửa. Dây điện cũ rất nguy hiểm. Phanh xe ô tô không phải việc nghiệp dư. Thiết bị y tế phải để cho chuyên gia. Lập luận ủng hộ việc sửa không phải lời kêu gọi cứ ôm đồm chuyên môn ở mọi ngành. Nó là lời kêu gọi cho một phán đoán nhỏ hàng ngày: 'cái này có thể sửa được; thử xem.'

Đôi khi người ta phản bác rằng sửa chữa là phí thời gian. Một cái ấm bốn mươi euro. Một giờ ở repair café tốn của bạn đúng một giờ đó. Nếu một giờ của bạn đáng giá hơn bốn mươi euro, thì thay mới là lý trí. Nhưng phản bác này giả định rằng một giờ ngồi sửa là một giờ trống. Không phải. Đó là một giờ học một điều mới, một giờ ở giữa những con người khác, và là một giờ — lặng lẽ, không cần ai tuyên bố — từ chối sống trong một thế giới mà cái gì cũng dùng một lần rồi vứt.

Rốt cuộc, cái ấm hầu như không còn là vấn đề chính.

questions.prompt: Where does the writer say the movement operates, and under what names?
options: Only in Britain — mending circles only
Repair cafés in the Netherlands, fixing workshops in Germany, mending circles in Britain
questions.explanation: 'Repair cafés in the Netherlands, fixing workshops in Germany, mending circles in Britain.'

questions.prompt: Who runs the sessions, according to the passage?
options: A pair of volunteers who used to be engineers or tailors, often with the owner watching
questions.explanation: 'A pair of volunteers, who used to work as engineers or tailors, tries to fix the thing, often with the owner watching.'

questions.prompt: What 'obvious benefits' does the writer list, and why does she say they are not enough?
options: Less waste, less money, less material — they convince editors but not young people who could order a new kettle online.
Better health, longer life, more sleep — they are scientifically wrong.
More friends, more food, more travel — they are too vague.
Higher prices — they are not really benefits.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 lists the three benefits and notes their limit.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's 'less obvious defence' of mending?
options: It teaches a habit of attention that the wider culture has begun to lose.
questions.explanation: 'Mending is not only about objects. It is also about a habit of attention that the wider culture has begun to lose.'

questions.prompt: Which of these four steps does the writer say mending REQUIRES?
options: Understanding how the thing was put together; looking inside; accepting that the broken part is small and the working part is large; briefly respecting the designer.
Buying tools, calling a friend, ordering parts, paying a fee.
Going to school, finishing a course, paying a teacher, taking an exam.
Posting a video online about the repair.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 stacks the four 'you must' steps.
questions.hint: Look for the four 'you must' sentences.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say people who start mending kettles often go on to mend?
options: Their relationships, old photographs, sentences in their own writing
questions.explanation: 'They start mending their relationships. They start mending old photographs. They start mending sentences in their own writing.'

questions.prompt: What limits does the writer admit?
options: Old electrical wiring, car brakes, medical devices — these are not for amateurs.
Only soft fabrics can be mended.
Only in winter can things be mended.

questions.prompt: How does the writer answer the objection that mending wastes time?
options: She accepts that mending is empty time.
She argues that the hour is time spent learning, in company, and quietly refusing to live in a disposable world.
She says time has no value.
She tells readers to skip work.
questions.hint: Look at the writer's defence of the 'hour.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by 'time spent — quietly, and without anyone announcing it — refusing to live in a world where everything is disposable'?
options: Mending is a small private stance against a throwaway culture, without slogans.
Mending should always be loud and shared on social media.
questions.explanation: The two parenthetical phrases 'quietly' and 'without anyone announcing it' say so directly.
questions.hint: Look at the modifying phrases inside the dashes.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the editorial NOT claiming?
options: That repair cafés produce real environmental benefits.
That amateurs should fix anything, including medical devices.
That mending spreads to other parts of life.
That replacing a kettle online is rational under certain conditions.
questions.explanation: The writer explicitly rejects amateur expertise in dangerous fields.

questions.prompt: What does 'The kettle, in the end, is almost beside the point' mean?
options: The kettle is the entire argument.
The real subject of the editorial was never the kettle — it was the habit of attention behind mending.
questions.explanation: The closing line names this directly.
questions.hint: Connect this line back to paragraph 3.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the editorial?
options: Mending is worth defending less for what it saves and more for the habit of attention it teaches — which spreads to the rest of life.
Young people are bad at fixing things.
questions.explanation: The writer offers a non-obvious defence centred on attention, not waste.
questions.hint: Look at how the writer reframes the argument away from the obvious benefits.

cloze.template:
There is a small movement in some European cities to {{b1}} things rather than replace them. The movement has different names — repair cafés in the Netherlands, fixing workshops in Germany, mending circles in Britain. The idea is unremarkable on its face. People meet on Saturday afternoons in church halls or community centres. Someone brings a broken {{b2}}. Someone else brings a torn coat. A pair of volunteers, who used to work as engineers or tailors, tries to fix the thing, often with the owner watching.

A defender of mending will list the obvious benefits. Less waste in {{b3}}. Less money spent. Less material pulled out of the earth for new products. All of these are real, and all of them have been made many times in the press. They are usually enough to convince an editor to commission an article about repair cafés. They are usually not enough to make a young person carry a kettle to a church hall instead of {{b4}} a new one online.

So let me offer a less obvious defence. Mending is not only about objects. It is also about a habit of {{b5}} that the wider culture has begun to lose. When you mend something, you must first understand how it was put together. You must look {{b6}}. You must accept that the broken part is small and the working part is large. You must respect, briefly, the person who designed it. None of this happens when you replace a thing.

This habit of attention spreads. People who mend their kettles often start mending other things. They start mending their {{b7}}. They start mending old photographs. They start mending sentences in their own writing. The mending café is a school whose first lesson is the kettle and whose later lessons are quieter.

There are limits, of course. Some things should not be mended. Old electrical wiring is dangerous. A car's brakes are not for {{b8}}. A medical device must be left to professionals. The argument for mending is not an argument for amateur expertise in every field. It is an argument for the small daily judgment: this object is mendable; let me {{b9}}.

The objection is sometimes raised that mending wastes time. A kettle costs forty euros. An hour at a repair café costs you that hour. If your hour is worth more than forty euros to you, replacing is rational. But this objection assumes that the hour spent mending is {{b10}} time. It is not. It is time spent learning, time spent in company, and time spent — quietly, and without anyone announcing it — refusing to live in a world where everything is disposable.

The kettle, in the end, is almost beside the {{b11}}.

blanks.explanation: Instead of ordering a new one online.
Car brakes are not for amateurs.
The objection assumes the hour is empty time.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The editorial argues that the kettle is almost beside the point — mending really teaches a kind of attention. Is there a small repair you have done recently — to an object, a habit, or a relationship — that changed how you approached the next thing you wanted to fix? What did the small repair actually teach you?
